speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] So-- Talk now. Now, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Dana Schwartz talking to Thomas Blatt. We're in Los Angeles and this is April 4, 1995. The interview is of course in English. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not yet, not yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were on the train. And you said to your friend, now, you've given me a way by talking to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the train come to the next stop he-- by the way, he informed another-- which we traveling which was another group, because we were about eight people, a few women included. So when the train had come to a stop, we went out. It was dark, of course. And it was fast. We mingled with another people, went to the beginning of the train, and jumped in another wagon. And we traveled up to the-- to the town of Kleparov. Kleparov is a suburb of Lwow. It's a big city. The agreement was in the beginning that we will not go by train, we will not cross Lwow by train. Because in the central station, they do very strict searches for Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we decided to get up-- it was planned to get up in Kleparov, go to Lwow by foot and, again, go to the-- on train and the suburb of-- of the Kleparov, Sknilow was the name. And I remember walking the streets of Lwow. I went through the [INAUDIBLE] terrible concentration camp on Janow Street. Janowska.

speaker: interviewee
text: Janowska. Janowska [INAUDIBLE] it was called. And I remember passing by and looking through the barbed wires and seeing the Jews. And it was a terrible feeling thinking, God, I could be one of them. But I passed without any trouble into Sknilow. And still if I remember, I still had 500 zlotys. So I thought it will take me, and I go on the train, I will get close to the border-- to the border. So I didn't need to the money anymore. So I bought some food. And finally, we stepped on the train. And we were traveling in the direction of the-- of the city called Stryj. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] About 3 kilometers before Stryj, the German conductor was checking the tickets. And we had with us this girl called Langart. And she had a-- a typical Semitic face. It was a typical, I mean dark color hair, the nose wasn't too short. And she, to hide her face, she did look out of the window pretending that she is looking at the scenery. When the German conductor did come asking for the tickets, she turned and showed her tickets. And she said [NON-ENGLISH]. If she wouldn't talk in German, maybe it will be OK. But she was a pretty girl and the German could have start to talk with her. And later she understood she should rather pretend not to understand German. And she started to back up that she doesn't understand. Anyway, it was immediately suspicious to the people traveling with us, mostly Ukrainian peasants. But the-- the German conductor, he wasn't suspicious. And he left. But soon after, Ukrainian militiamen arrived and checking her out. At that point, I made a mistake. If I would act like all my neighbors, oh, they caught a Jew, they will kill her, this-- it will be OK. But you see I, up until now, if I'm in an embarrassing situation, I start to sweat, perspire. At that point, two of us getting red, perspiring, and I didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took a newspaper I did want to hide my face. And I hold the paper pretending that I'm reading. And probably I did look-- I did look like a sore thumb, standing-- sticking out between the all other people acting a completely different way. Because, pretty soon, the paper was ripped from my face. And I see. I look up, and there's a Ukrainian militiaman. Your papers. He took the papers. What's your name? And-- and the way I didn't have time to learn to memorize the name. The name was [INAUDIBLE]. So I said [INAUDIBLE]. But they call me [INAUDIBLE]. Are you a Jew? I say, no. You're Jewish? No. No. My mouth said no, but my face said yes. He told me to get up. You're not Jewish? No. Come with me. He took me to the toilet and told me to pull down the pants. And I pulled down the pants. He looks close and still can't recognize. What-- why he wasn't able to recognize? Why? Because weeks before, I used to-- I tried to correct this, but the-- [INAUDIBLE] the-- the-- they do-- I tried to correct the circumcision. How?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I tied it with a string and pushed it, different things I'd done. The result was that everything was swollen. So he wasn't able to recognize if I'm a Jew or not. But it was obvious to him that some manipulation was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he called another guard, Ukrainian. And he started to look. And he probably wasn't either well educated in-- in the-- in the kind-- in this kind of situation. But he decided I must be a Jew where they assessed the sight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the train stopped on the city, on the train station Stryj, he told me to go down. And actually, they collected all of us. And they took us to the militia over there. Every train station had a little police office. And there was a SS man and Ukrainians. They told us to undress, which we did. And they searched our clothing. And later, one Ukrainian hit me right in the throat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not in the face, in the throat, so I wasn't able to catch air. And the SS man, surprisingly, start to yell at him. He shouldn't beat us. The officer of SS, yeah, yelled at him. If not for the SS man, probably be beaten very badly. And later, they tell us to undress. And they put us to a-- they had a little arrest-- a little jail in the train station. They told us to go in, and we went in, all the men. The women went to another place, maybe another cell. And we were sitting over there. It was dark. A little bit light did come from a window, which was on the opposite end from the door, very high. And about a half an hour later, the door-- the door opened a little bit, a crack. And the Ukrainian policeman said, listen guys, you will die anyway. But I could give you a good advice if you give me some money. He probably, from experience, was aware that some of us still maybe hidden. Rappaport had in his glass case, 5 gold ruble-- ruble-- 5 ruble gold pieces. So he grabbed-- he did pick up one he did give him. And he said, at that point, the guard opened wider the door. So light had come in. He pointed at this window. Now, we could see it very well. On the bars on the window, something was hanging, some piece of clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the guard said, you see this window? In this window, yesterday a Jew, like we, like you are here, hanged himself. And do the same thing, it will be better for you. Let one hanged another one until the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we looked. And over there was long underwear was hanging on the-- on the-- on the bars. Probably, he said it true that the day before somebody hanged themself. So he closed the door again. We didn't do-- we didn't try to hang ourselves. A few hours later, the-- the door opens again, and some policeman in civil suit, they come with guns and told us if we moved they will kill us. We should go two abreast straight forward. And they led us through the streets of Stryj We were surrounded by the civil guards, criminal police, as I find out later. And we were taken to the big jail in Stryj, a regular jail. And I remember they put us on the third floor. And the cell number was 27. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were in this cell, eight Jews, two Ukrainians. One was held. And we didn't know if he was a Jew or not. We weren't able to verify it. I don't know why. And then-- and one Czech, Jalinek was his name. And the door closed, and here we are. I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] across when I looked down from the third floor, across was another little jail, another building, much smaller. Our building has, I think, four floors. But I was sitting in jail, about four floors. But opposite was a smaller building with two floors only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it in the square? In Stryj, was it part of the square?

speaker: interviewee
text: The jail?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the jail in the main square?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I went in. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I noticed that once in awhile, when I look through the window down, small groups of people, which were obvious Jews, were put in this jail across from my place. That's all. Now, I will tell you something which sounds unbelievable, but is forever [INAUDIBLE] to. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We was-- we were a few people. The Ukrainian did behave wonderful. He was so good when food would come, he did give half to me and to my friend Wolf. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And night time, we did put four straw mattresses, straw sacks-- it's not like a mattress in America-- and we slept on it. Because [INAUDIBLE] was the order to go asleep. And nobody could fall asleep anyway. So the rule was that each one of us must tell the story of his life. So every day was something else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember we had a guard. We had a-- a-- fellow, he was a lawyer. And he was able to imitate different instruments, fiddle, or trumpet. So I remember we had a guard who did give us extra soup. He promised us extra soup if, the next day, if he will-- he will imitate a fiddle. So anyway, so one night we had this and another night something else. So when my turn had come to tell my story, it was short of a thing, [INAUDIBLE] and I'm here. But when the turn was to Alvin Lippman, Alvin Lippman's story, something strange did come out. His story-- he told us a story. He was [INAUDIBLE] in the army in Germany when Hitler conducted his [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Means?

speaker: interviewee
text: His little revolt when he was arrested later. And General [? Ludendorff ?] was leading a platoon of soldiers. He-- he did march with Hitler. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he as a young [INAUDIBLE], Alvin Lippman, was ordered to go with the-- with the soldiers by Ludendorff. And later, when the [NON-ENGLISH] failed, of course, some were wounded. Hitler was arrested. And the soldiers were let to go. They let them go. Anyway, after this [NON-ENGLISH], Hitler-- now, Hitler probably didn't do it. But Hitler's chancellery sent out a letter of thanks, of appreciation, for the first fighters for the [INAUDIBLE], [? NSP, ?] the Social Nationalist Organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Party.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, party. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so he received such a letter signed by Hitler. And later, when he did get older, he married a girl, a Jewish girl. And he had two kids. Now, when the-- when the years progressed, of course, when the war started with Poland, he was called to the army. And he advanced to the rank of a captain. At that time, Jewish wives of German soldiers were protected, because their husband is in the army. But he-- he had a high position, a captain. So to tell him take a divorce, you have a chance to grow and return [INAUDIBLE]. Or, we must let you go from the army. So he decided he wanted to go back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: He, himself, was a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was not a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a German. He married a-- a Jewish girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they asked they should divorce. He didn't want to. So he went back to [INAUDIBLE]. And the moment they he was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they let him out from the army, his family wasn't protected. So the next what's happened his wife and children were order to be resettled to Poland. So he didn't want to let them go by themselves. He did go with them to Poland. In Poland, he settled in the city of Zamosc, where the Germans made him the head of the Jewish Police. And I verified this after the war. And it's true. They may made him the head of the Jewish Police. And the time did come that his wife and children were liquidated to ghetto. And he was alone. He was still a German, so they didn't touch him. So he was trying to go to Czechoslovakia. And they arrested him in Stryj in the train, [INAUDIBLE] verify. He told the story. The next day, I remember, we were in one room like this. You could hear it. The lawyer told him, listen, Mr.-- Mr. Lippman, if I would be in your place, I wouldn't sit a minute in the jail. And they were-- they were asking the guard for the papers, for some paper and a pencil. And the way the lawyer dictate him, they wrote a letter to Governor Hanks-- Frank's office in Krakow. It didn't take long. Probably, the letter didn't go to Krakow, to Frank. Probably, the gestapo in the jail did in-- get the letter, because it took two days. And we heard some steps in the hallway. And the steps stopped in front of our door. And the door opened, and there were two SS officers. And they look around. And they say, Herr-- Herr Alvin Lippman. Herr, that sounded already optimistic. Because to a Jew, they never said that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mister, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. Take your stuff and come with us. And he grabbed his stuff, and the SS are looking around. And they asked the Kommandant-- the Kommandant-- the Kommandant for the jail was a Pole. His same was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: They ask, Herr Kommandant, [NON-ENGLISH]? What kind-- what people-- what kind of people are here? We see more people. Oh, they're people which belong to the criminal police. And what do you mean criminal? Criminal police. So they point a finger at me, who's he? A Jew. Who's he? A Jew. He? Ukrainian. What is him? No, he is-- we don't know really if he's a Pole or a Jew. So they made a very fast, an order. He's a Jew. The Ukrainian, let him go. And the order us to-- to deliver us to the Jewish section of the jail, which was down. I mentioned this little building. And they left with-- with Alvin Lippman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And an hour later, the guards did come, took us-- took us down and took us to the next jail. The next jail, it wasn't a good place. Me and Wolf and the rest of the Jews in our group, were pushed in a big room, was a huge room, about a 100 people. It was terrible. It was stinky. There was a-- a-- herring-- a barrel from herring, and this was our toilet. It was overflowing. But I forgot to tell you this. In the jail, I had a good friend. He was photograph-- a photographer in the city of Stryj. His name was Zellinger. And this Zellinger took a liking of me. And he talked with me. And they-- and in that cell 27, the Jews were allowed to get food parcels from the ghetto. So this Zellinger had a wife in the ghetto. And she delivered food parcels. And he helped me, too. So I wasn't hungry. And now, when we were transferred to the other jail, he for some reason-- maybe the guard wasn't bribed-- they took him to another cell. And I was in this cell. In the cell 27, it was fairly clean, neat. Here, it was so terrible. I was afraid to even to sit down, because I could see the insects and everything else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a bunch of us standing there with Wolf. And about an hour later the doors opened and a guy called, the guard called, Vlad? Where's Vlad? And Schneidmesser? Wolf was-- his name was Schneidmesser. And they called us out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wolf was close to the door. And he left. I, at that time, not to sit in the dirt, I was, it was an old jail with very thick walls. And where the window was, was about a 40 feet frame. I was sitting on the frame and holding onto-- with my hand to the bar. So two had to jump down, two people to move away. The guard was impatient and closed the door. And finally I went down, door was closed. And I remember hitting with my forehead, the-- the door. Please, I went-- I want out. I didn't know what I was saying, because the door opened. I was beaten up, but of I was taken out. He beat me, the guard was [? still ?] take me and took me to another cell where Zellinger was. It looked like Zellinger bribed them. [INAUDIBLE] And it was maybe more people than down, than where I was. But it was clean. And there was a lot of food. I remember the first jail was a Ukrainian. He wasn't a Jew, a Ukrainian peasant. He was, they caught him hiding Jews. And I remember he was sitting and praying. He crossed himself all the time. So probably he was killed in [INAUDIBLE] together with Jews. And over there in this cell where I was now, was the more intellectuals, which were able to bribe the guards, because business was going on. I remember they put with a rope, they put down a basket with money through the-- through the-- through the bars. And in turn, they pulled up food, bread, sausage for the Polish prisoners, they'd give in exchange. In the evening, there was some poetry. They did read poetry. It was-- they were intellectuals. And the atmosphere was much better. And we were forced every day to take down our shirts and do delousing, look for insects. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One day, a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a prisoner did get a piece of bread from-- from the home, from the ghetto. So he told me, [INAUDIBLE], I will pick you up. And you put it-- there was a oven there-- put the bread in the oven on top.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he picked me up. And I put away the bread. And when he picked me up I put the bread, I  notice a long, some long boxes, like a carton of cigarettes here. And when I went down, I went to Zellinger and told him, Mr. Zellinger, something is in the oven. Could you pick me up again? He picked me up. And I picked down the package. And it was cigarettes. You know what cigarettes are in a jail? I was a millionaire immediately on the spot. I didn't smoke, so I sell the cigarettes. And I was-- I did give Zellinger some. And I had some money at that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: The cigarettes probably, they were Russian cigarettes, probability left from the Russian time when the Russians were in Lwow for some prisoners. Probably called them up so fast that they killed them. Now, some people, they start to be sick. And we were afraid that the Nazis will find out they are sick. And we  were aware what kind of medicine the Nazis have. They would have shot us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we did try when, every day in the morning we had the roll call. And because there was so many people, one hold another one. The sick people, they hold them between themselves. And the guard wasn't able to notice that the people can't stand on their feet.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there were letters out to the Judenrat in Stryj to help us. As so happened, one evening Zellinger received a little letter from the guard. The guards were bribed to bring letters from the people in the ghetto. And he called me. Zellinger said, [INAUDIBLE], listen, tomorrow, or maybe after tomorrow, a doctor will come from the ghetto and will ask for sick people. And you tell him that you're sick. When you will be outside in the hospital, I will be able to maybe do something for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: What's happened? The jail didn't have a hospital. So the hospital was in the ghetto. So I said, Mr. Zellinger, they will kill us if you say we are sick. Don't worry. If my wife told me, it's OK. They had connections probably. And so it happened a few days later a doctor did come with his assistant. And the guards opened the door. And he yells, who is sick? I did come to him, I'm sick. So he did give about, most of the people were afraid. But about seven people maybe agreed. Some were obviously laying around, you can-- so they were sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took a thermometer. When I took my thermometer, I went in the corner and I rubbed it against the shirt. I rubbed it so hard that it jumped to the end, I should be dying at that fever. So I was trying to turn it down. And when it knocked down, it was-- the silver was interrupted in a few places. I was panicking, but finally I managed to make it [INAUDIBLE]. And I didn't want to touch it. I went back to the doctor, gave him the thermometer. The doctor looked at me. He said, oh, I could take only 20 people. You don't look too sick. So I didn't know what to do. And I remember my brother in the ghetto was sick. He had typhus. And I remember he had red spots. So I punched my chest to make red spots. But this time, I didn't go to the doctor. I did go to his assistant, I took 20 zlotys which I had from the cigarettes, did give him the 20 zlotys and told him, look, I am sick really. And he motioned-- he motioned me to the people who were already selected to be taken out. In this way, I find myself in a group of 20 people. And-- and later the gates open, they took us out into street to the streets of Stryj. It was cold, it was winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: '4-- I don't-- Now, I don't remember. Was it at the end of-- oh, maybe this will give me an idea. A short time before, one of the prisoners did get a package with food. And in a-- in the newspaper, it talked about [INAUDIBLE] surrending-- surrounding-- surrending-- surrrending--

speaker: interviewer
text: Surrendering.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in-- in Russia. So if I could find a date, I would know exactly. And this was maybe a week later. We were so happy that the German armies defeated. I still remember what kind of feeling we had. Now, we in the streets. And I decided to run. We had come to some [INAUDIBLE] streets, I will run. We were guarded, but not by Germans. We were guarded by Jewish police. And the Jewish police considered us sick. So no one actual-- in the front, they were flirting with some girls. And we were lead to the-- and I thought I will jump to a side street. But now, near me-- close to me was a man maybe 20 some years old. And he did look healthy to me, too. So I thought maybe he want to escape, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we start to talk. And he said, yes, but not now. My father's in the ghetto. We go to the hospital. My father will come, give us money, clothing. And we will manage. So I said, OK, doesn't hurt. So we went-- they took us to the hospital. It was a Jewish hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all the rooms were people from the ghetto. But one room we selected for the prisoners. And where the windows were, there was Jewish guard policemen and where the door was a Jewish policeman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the door leading to another rooms where the sick people from the city was, there were-- there was also a policeman. So they were responsible for their lives. We were very, very well guarded otherwise. Now, I'm healthy. They told us to undress. I undressed, but instead to give the clothing to disinfection, I put it into my mattress. I managed to hide it and just waiting for the occasion to run away. Now, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was healthy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't want to sleep with the-- with the sick people. There were two people to one bed. So I decide I will sleep on chairs. I put chairs together and slept in the chairs. But in-- in the evening, the Jewish doctor did come. A young people-- person, he did want to show off. He told me to get out and go to bed. So I went to the bed with a sick person.
